domain:
In the context of a theater organization, various plays are performed in one or more theaters, each identified by its own location, for example "The Northwest Theater". A play may have multiple scheduled sessions, and each session occurs on a scheduled date. Plays are identified by a unique name and is either a musical or an opera. Furthermore, each play may take place during the day or at night.

Each play is written by one or more authors, who are uniquely identified by name. Plays involve participants each also identified by name, and the same participant may act in multiple plays. For each play session, the starting time and an ending time is recorded, and participants can act in one or more sessions. Both authors and participant can be the same person, who may write a play and participate in its performance.

questions.question:
Should theater be modeled as a separate class associated with Play, or as a simple representation within Play?

For example:
1. Theater is a separate class with attributes: location. This allows users to query and filter by individual theater properties.
2. Theater is modeled as a simple represntation in Play. This is suitable when theater details are not needed.
Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The Theater is a separate class with its own attributes.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The theater is a simple representation in Play.
questions.selected: Option 1: The Theater is a separate class with its own attributes.

questions.question:
Should location be modeled as a separate class associated with Theater, or remain as a simple representation?

For example:
1. Location is a separate class with its own attributes. This allows users to query and filter by individual location properties.
2. Location is modeled as a simple representation in Theater. This is suitable when location details are not needed.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: The Location is a separate class with its own attributes.
questions.option_2: Option 2: The location is a simple representation in Theater.
questions.selected: Option 2: The location is a simple representation in Theater.

questions.question:
Should the location in theater be modeled as a an attribute which allows free text input, or as an enumeration with predefined literals?

For example:
1. Use location as string attribute in theater, which allows any text value.
2. Use location as an enumeration which uses predefined values in literals: the northwest theater.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: String attribute that allows any text value for location.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Enumeration location with predefined values: literal 1, literal 2 and literal 3.
questions.selected: Option 1: String attribute that allows any text value for location.

questions.question:
What is the minimum cardinality of Play in the relationship with Theater?

For example:
1. Minimum cardinality of zero, a theater can exist without any associated play.
2. Minimum cardinality of one, a theater must have at least one associated play.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Theater can exist without Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: One as minimum cardinality, Theater must have at least one Play.
questions.selected: Option 1: Zero as minimum cardinality, Theater can exist without Play.

questions.question:
What is the maximum cardinality of Play in the relationship with Theater?

For example:
1. Maximum cardinality of one, a theater is associated with exactly one play.
2. Maximum cardinality of many, a theater can be associated with multiple plays.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: One as maximun cardinality, each theater links to one play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each theater can link to multiple plays.
questions.selected: Option 2: Many as maximun cardinality, each theater can link to multiple plays.

questions.question:
Should the relationship between Play and Participant be modeled as a composition or as an association?

For example:
1. Use composition, Participant would depend on Play for its existence.
2. Use association, Participant could exist independently of Play, and can also be linked to multiple plays.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Composition is needed because class Participant depends on Play.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Participant exists independently of Play.
questions.selected: Option 2: Association is sufficient because class Participant exists independently of Play.

questions.question:
Should musical and opera be represented as separate subclasses of Play, or as instances of Play distinguished by an enumeration attribute?
Consider whether these types have distinct attributes or behavior.

For example:
1. Musical and Opera are modeled as separate subclasses of Play. Each subclass can have its own specific attributes and methods.
2. A single Play class with a Type enumeration attribute with literals: musical or opera. All types share the same structure.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Use inheritance, create separate subclasses for musical and opera.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Use enumeration Play type with with literals: musical or opera.
questions.selected: Option 1: Use inheritance, create separate subclasses for musical and opera.

questions.question:
Should a generalization hierarchy be introduced between classes Participant and Author that share the following attributes: name?

For example:
1. Use the attributes name separated. The classes Participant and Author maintains independent attributes.
2. Use a common superclass Person containing the shared attributes name. This removes duplication in subclasses Participant and Author.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Generalization is not needed, keep attributes (name) duplicated in each class.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Generalization is needed, create Person superclass with shared attributes, Participant and Author as subclasses.
questions.selected: Option 2: Generalization is needed, create Person superclass with shared attributes, Participant and Author as subclasses.

questions.question:
Should PlaySession be modeled as an association class linking Play and Participant, or as a regular class with two separate associations?

For example:
1. Association class: PlaySession exists only as part of the relationship between Play and Participant. A play session cannot exist without the link between Play and Participant.
2. Regular class: PlaySession is a class that has links to Play and Participant. In this case multiple play sessions can exist for every Play and Participant.


Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Association class, PlaySession as association class linking Play and Participant.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Regular class, PlaySession as separate class with two associations.
questions.selected: Option 1: Association class, PlaySession as association class linking Play and Participant.

questions.question:
Should Person be modeled as an abstract class or as a concrete class?
Consider whether Person can be instantiated directly, or only through its subclasses (Participant or Author).

For example:
1. Concrete class Person that can be instantiated. Class people can exist independently.
2. Abstract class Person that cannot be instantiated. Every person must be a specific type participant or author.

Which option is better for you?
questions.option_1: Option 1: Concrete class Person that can be instantiated directly.
questions.option_2: Option 2: Abstract class Person, where only the subclasses can be instantiated.
questions.selected: Option 2: Abstract class Person, where only the subclasses can be instantiated.
